free_text: A study of (3He,n)reactions on 24Mg, 28Si and 32S.
The SUNI 6 MeV Van de Graaff accelerator and a
time-of-flight neutron spectrometer.
The neutron detector consists of a glass cell
12 cm*4 cm filled with NE213 liquid scintillator. This
cell is viewed by a 12.5 cm, 58 AVP photomultiplier.A
fast pulse is taken from the 12th dynode for timing
purposes. Pulse shape discrimination was applied to
reject gamma events in the scintillator.
Digitizing error
Digitizing error
Error from the graphic bars
The most important additional sources of error in the
angular distributions are uncertainties in the relative
efficiency of the detector and the estimation of
backgrounds. These are estimated to be less than 15%.
A 99.93% enriched 24MgO target was obtained from AERE,
Harwell. It was deposited on a 0.01 cm tantalum backing
to a nominal thickness of 67 mu-g/cm2. The total
thickness of the 24MgO target was determined from the
measured width of a narrow resonance in the 24Mg(a,g)
reaction at 3.21 MeV. This was converted to a thickness
of 22 keV for 3He at 5.6 MeV.
Fig.9
Natural silicon targets were made by vacuum evaporation
on to 0.25 mm tantalum backing. The thickness of the Si
target used at 5.6 MeV was derived from the measured
width of a narrow resonance in the reaction
30Si(a,g)34S at 3.25 MeV. This yielded a thickness of
83 keV for 3He at 5.6 MeV. The 28Si content was
70 mu-g/cm2.
Fig.10
Thin natural CdS targets were made by vacuum
evaporation on to 0.25 mm tantalum backing. The total
thickness of these targets could only be estimated.
Fig.11
The amount of 12C in the target was determined by
comparison with the yield from a 12C target of known
thickness (180+-10 mu-g/cm2) and using the known (to
within 15%) cross sections for the 12C(He3,n)14O
reaction at 5.6 MeV.
Fig.12
The 24MgO target content was 5 and 23 mu-g/cm2.
